name: Hard Money Loans
h1: Hard Money Loans for Real Estate Investors -- Complete Guide
description: Everything you need to know about hard money loans for real estate investing. Rates, terms, qualifications, top lenders, and how to apply.
keywords: hard money loans, hard money lender, fix and flip financing, hard money loan rates, hard money real estate, asset based lending
overview: Hard money loans are short-term, asset-based loans secured by real estate. Unlike conventional mortgages that evaluate your income and credit history, hard money lenders focus primarily on the property's value and potential. They are most commonly used by fix-and-flip investors, developers, and wholesalers who need fast capital to close deals. Funding can happen in as little as 7 days, making them ideal for competitive markets where speed is everything. The trade-off is higher interest rates and origination fees compared to traditional financing.
notGoodFor: Long-term buy-and-hold rentals
Owner-occupied primary residences
Tight-margin deals with thin spreads
Borrowers who need low monthly payments
qualificationRequirements: Property as collateral (primary qualification factor)
10-25% down payment or equity
Detailed rehab budget and scope of work
Clear exit strategy (sell or refinance)
Experience preferred but not always required
Proof of funds for down payment and reserves

pros: Fast closing in 7-14 days
Based on property value, not borrower credit
Flexible and negotiable terms
No income verification typically needed
Can finance properties in poor condition
cons: High interest rates (10-14%)
Origination points add 2-4% upfront cost
Short term only -- must refinance or sell quickly
Higher risk of foreclosure if project stalls
Monthly payments can be substantial

howToApply: Identify 3-5 hard money lenders in your target market and compare terms
Prepare your deal package: property details, purchase price, ARV, rehab budget, and exit strategy
Submit applications to multiple lenders for competitive term sheets
Review and negotiate terms -- focus on rate, points, LTV, and prepayment penalties
Get pre-approval letter and finalize your term sheet
Close at a title company with standard real estate closing procedures

name: Private Money Lending
h1: Private Money Lending for Real Estate -- How to Find & Structure Deals
description: Complete guide to private money lending for real estate investors. Learn how to find private lenders, structure deals, and negotiate terms.
keywords: private money lending, private lender real estate, private money loans, private real estate financing, private investor loans
overview: Private money lending involves borrowing from individuals rather than institutions. These are typically high-net-worth individuals, family members, friends, or business associates who want to earn returns on their capital by lending it secured against real estate. Unlike hard money lenders who operate as businesses with standardized terms, private money deals are fully negotiable and relationship-driven. Private money is often the most flexible financing available to real estate investors, but it requires strong networking skills and the ability to build trust.
bestFor: Projects that don't fit institutional criteria
Repeat investors building long-term lending relationships
notGoodFor: New investors with no track record
Those uncomfortable with personal financial relationships
Deals requiring institutional-grade documentation
Borrowers who need standardized, repeatable processes
qualificationRequirements: Strong personal relationship or referral to lender
Clear and compelling deal presentation
Documented exit strategy
Willingness to secure loan with property deed of trust
Track record of successful deals (preferred)
Transparent communication about risks

pros: Fully negotiable terms -- rate, points, LTV, term
Can close faster than any other lending type
No credit score requirements
Flexible on property condition and type
Can structure interest-only or deferred payments
cons: Requires strong networking and relationship skills
Limited scalability -- depends on individual lenders
Can strain personal relationships if deal goes wrong
Less legal structure than institutional loans
Finding reliable private lenders takes time

howToApply: Build your network: attend REI meetups, join investor groups, and connect with high-net-worth individuals
Prepare a professional deal presentation with property details, financials, and exit strategy
Present the opportunity clearly, emphasizing the security (real estate collateral) and returns
Negotiate terms that work for both parties -- be fair and transparent
Use a real estate attorney to draft a proper promissory note and deed of trust
Close through a title company to protect both parties

name: DSCR Loans
h1: DSCR Loans Explained -- Qualify Based on Rental Income, Not Your W-2
description: Complete guide to DSCR loans for rental property investors. Learn DSCR requirements, how to qualify, top lenders, and when DSCR loans make sense.
keywords: DSCR loans, DSCR loan requirements, debt service coverage ratio loan, DSCR rental property, DSCR mortgage, no income verification loan
overview: DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio) loans allow real estate investors to qualify based on the property's rental income rather than their personal income. The lender evaluates whether the property's rental revenue covers the mortgage payment, taxes, insurance, and HOA fees. A DSCR of 1.0 means the property breaks even; most lenders require 1.0-1.25. DSCR loans are perfect for self-employed investors, those with complex tax returns, or anyone building a portfolio who doesn't want to use personal income documentation. They have become the go-to loan product for buy-and-hold investors since 2020.
bestFor: Portfolio builders scaling past 4-10 conventional loans
notGoodFor: Fix and flip projects (no rental income)
Properties with negative cash flow
Vacant properties with no rental history
Primary residence purchases
qualificationRequirements: Property DSCR of 1.0-1.25 minimum (rental income / PITIA)
20-25% down payment
Credit score of 660+ (680+ for best rates)
6+ months cash reserves
Appraisal with rental income analysis (Form 1007 or rent schedule)
Property must be investment (non-owner-occupied)

pros: No personal income verification required
No DTI ratio calculation
Can close in LLC or entity name
30-year fixed terms available
Scalable -- no limit on number of DSCR loans
Faster approval than conventional
cons: Higher rates than conventional (7-9% vs 6-7%)
Requires 20-25% down payment
Prepayment penalties are common (3-5 years)
Property must cash flow at or above breakeven
Higher credit score requirements than hard money

howToApply: Run your numbers: calculate the property's DSCR (monthly rent / monthly PITIA)
Get a rent estimate or appraisal with rental analysis for the target property
Shop 3-5 DSCR lenders and compare rates, LTV, prepayment penalties, and reserve requirements
Submit your application with property details, rent projections, and entity documentation
Complete appraisal and underwriting (typically 2-3 weeks)
Close at title company -- fund through your entity if desired

name: Conventional Investment Loans
h1: Conventional Investment Loans -- Best Rates for Rental Properties
description: Guide to conventional investment property loans. Requirements, rates, down payments, and how to qualify for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac financing.
keywords: conventional investment loan, investment property mortgage, rental property loan, Fannie Mae investment, Freddie Mac rental, conventional mortgage investment property
overview: Conventional investment loans are standard mortgages backed by Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, adapted for non-owner-occupied properties. They offer the lowest interest rates available for investment properties, but come with strict qualification requirements including full income documentation, debt-to-income ratio limits, and a cap of 10 financed properties per borrower. For investors with strong W-2 income, good credit, and sufficient reserves, conventional loans provide the best long-term financing terms in the market.
bestFor: W-2 employees with strong documented income
Maximizing cash flow with lowest rate
notGoodFor: Self-employed with complex tax returns
Investors with 10+ financed properties
Properties needing major rehab
Fast closings (under 21 days)
LLC or entity purchases
qualificationRequirements: DTI ratio under 45% (including new property)
Credit score 620+ (720+ for best rates and terms)
2 years tax returns and W-2s or full income documentation
6 months reserves per property (PITIA)
15-25% down payment (25% for best rates)
Maximum 10 financed properties (Fannie Mae)

pros: Lowest interest rates for investment properties
No prepayment penalties
30-year fixed terms for predictable payments
No seasoning requirements for refinance
Widely available from any mortgage lender
cons: Strict income documentation requirements
DTI limits can be restrictive
Cap of 10 financed properties per borrower
Cannot close in LLC name (personal guarantee required)
Slow closing (30-45 days)
Higher down payment than primary residence (15-25%)

howToApply: Check your credit score and DTI ratio before applying
Gather 2 years of tax returns, W-2s, pay stubs, and bank statements
Calculate reserves needed: 6 months PITIA per financed property
Shop rates with 3-5 lenders including local banks, credit unions, and online lenders
Get pre-approved and lock your rate when you find a property
Complete full underwriting, appraisal, and close at title company

name: FHA House Hacking
h1: FHA House Hacking -- Start Investing with 3.5% Down
description: How to use FHA loans for house hacking. Buy a multi-unit property with 3.5% down, live in one unit, and rent the others to cover your mortgage.
keywords: FHA house hacking, house hacking FHA loan, FHA multi unit, FHA 3.5 down investment, house hack strategy, FHA duplex financing
overview: FHA house hacking is the strategy of using an FHA loan (3.5% down payment) to purchase a multi-unit property (2-4 units), living in one unit as your primary residence, and renting out the remaining units. The rental income from the other units can partially or fully cover your mortgage payment, effectively allowing you to live for free while building equity and cash flow. This is widely considered the single best strategy for new investors to get started in real estate with minimal capital. FHA allows you to count 75% of projected rental income toward your qualifying income.
bestFor: Those willing to live in their investment
Investors in markets with affordable multi-units
Building a rental portfolio starting from zero
notGoodFor: Investors who don't want to live on-site
Markets where multi-units are too expensive
Those needing more than 4 units
Investors wanting to close in an LLC
Properties that need major rehab before occupancy
qualificationRequirements: Credit score 580+ (3.5% down) or 500-579 (10% down)
Must live in one unit as primary residence for 12 months
Property must be 1-4 units
DTI ratio under 43% (can go to 50% with compensating factors)
Stable employment history (2 years)
FHA mortgage insurance premium (MIP) required

pros: Only 3.5% down payment -- lowest in the market
Can count 75% of rental income for qualification
Lower credit score requirements than conventional
30-year fixed rate locks in predictable costs
Builds equity and cash flow simultaneously
Rental income can cover entire mortgage
cons: Must live in the property for at least 12 months
FHA mortgage insurance adds to monthly cost
Property condition requirements (FHA appraisal standards)
Loan limits vary by county -- may not cover expensive markets
Only one FHA loan at a time (generally)
Multi-unit properties can be harder to find

howToApply: Check FHA loan limits in your target county (varies by area and unit count)
Get pre-approved with an FHA-approved lender
Search for 2-4 unit properties in your target market
Analyze the deal: calculate rental income vs. PITIA to ensure positive cash flow
Make an offer, complete FHA appraisal (property must meet FHA minimum standards)
Close and move into one unit within 60 days -- rent remaining units

name: Seller Financing
h1: Seller Financing for Real Estate -- How to Buy Properties Without Banks
description: Complete guide to seller financing in real estate. Learn how to negotiate, structure, and close seller-financed deals without bank involvement.
keywords: seller financing, owner financing, seller carry back, seller financing real estate, owner financed homes, creative financing real estate
overview: Seller financing (also called owner financing or seller carry-back) is when the property seller acts as the lender instead of a bank. The buyer makes payments directly to the seller based on negotiated terms -- interest rate, down payment, loan term, and balloon date. No bank or institutional lender is involved. This strategy is powerful for investors because the terms are fully negotiable, closings can happen quickly, and properties that can't qualify for traditional financing can still be purchased. Seller financing is one of the most underused and powerful creative financing strategies in real estate.
bestFor: Properties that can't qualify for bank financing
Motivated sellers who want monthly income
Investors with limited credit or documentation
Deals where speed and flexibility matter
Building a portfolio without bank limits
notGoodFor: Sellers who need full cash at closing
Properties with existing mortgage (unless subject-to)
Buyers who want the lowest possible interest rate
Deals requiring title insurance complications
qualificationRequirements: Willing seller who owns property free and clear (or with low balance)
Negotiated down payment (typically 5-20%)
Mutually agreed terms documented in promissory note
Title search to confirm clear title
Real estate attorney to draft documents
No bank qualification required

pros: No bank qualification needed
Terms are 100% negotiable
Can close in 7-14 days
Lower closing costs than bank loans
Can buy properties in any condition
No limit on number of seller-financed deals
cons: Hard to find willing sellers
Balloon payments create refinance pressure
Sellers may charge above-market rates
Less legal protection than institutional loans
Due-on-sale clause risk on existing mortgages
Requires strong negotiation skills

howToApply: Target motivated sellers: tired landlords, inherited properties, free-and-clear owners
Present seller financing as a win-win: monthly income for seller, flexible terms for buyer
Negotiate key terms: purchase price, down payment, interest rate, term, and balloon date
Hire a real estate attorney to draft the promissory note and deed of trust/mortgage
Complete title search and obtain title insurance
Close at title company -- deed transfers to buyer, seller holds note
topLenders: The property seller (no institutional lender involved)
Real estate attorneys for document preparation

name: Subject-To Financing
h1: Subject-To Financing -- Take Over Existing Mortgages for Real Estate Deals
description: Learn how subject-to financing works in real estate. Buy properties by taking over the seller's existing mortgage without qualifying for a new loan.
keywords: subject to financing, subject to real estate, subject to mortgage, buying subject to, sub to deals, creative real estate financing
overview: Subject-to financing means purchasing a property 'subject to' the existing mortgage remaining in place. The deed transfers to the buyer, but the seller's mortgage stays in the seller's name and the buyer makes the payments. This allows investors to acquire properties with little to no money down while benefiting from the seller's existing loan terms -- which may have a lower interest rate than anything available today. Subject-to is one of the most powerful creative financing strategies, but it requires understanding the due-on-sale clause risk and proper legal documentation.
bestFor: Sellers needing quick relief from mortgage
Investors focused on cash flow from day one
notGoodFor: Risk-averse investors (due-on-sale clause)
Properties with little or no equity
Sellers who need cash at closing
Markets with declining values
Investors unfamiliar with creative finance legal requirements

typicalTerms.downPayment: $0-10,000 typical (to seller for equity or arrears)

qualificationRequirements: No bank qualification required -- you take over existing loan
Seller agreement to leave mortgage in their name
Enough cash to cover seller's equity position or back payments
Real estate attorney for proper documentation
Understanding of due-on-sale clause risks
Plan to service the existing mortgage payments reliably

pros: No bank qualification or credit check
Assume potentially low interest rates from prior era
Little to no money down in many cases
Fast closing in 7-14 days
No origination fees or points
Immediate cash flow from existing low-rate debt
cons: Due-on-sale clause risk (bank can call loan due)
Legal complexity requires experienced attorney
Seller's credit is tied to your payment performance
Insurance and title considerations are complex
Not widely understood by sellers or agents
Must manage payments on loan in someone else's name

howToApply: Find motivated sellers: pre-foreclosure, divorce, relocation, inherited properties
Verify existing loan details: balance, rate, payment, term remaining, lender
Negotiate terms: purchase price, cash to seller, who pays arrears if any
Hire a real estate attorney experienced in subject-to transactions
Execute proper documentation: purchase agreement, deed, authorization to release information
Record deed at county recorder, set up loan payment servicing, insure the property
topLenders: No institutional lender involved -- existing mortgage is assumed
Real estate attorneys for transaction structuring
Title companies for deed recording and closing

name: Transactional Funding
h1: Transactional Funding for Wholesalers -- Close Double Closes Same Day
description: How transactional funding works for real estate wholesalers. Close double-close deals with short-term capital, same-day or next-day funding.
keywords: transactional funding, double close funding, wholesale real estate funding, same day close, transactional lending, wholesale deal funding
overview: Transactional funding is ultra-short-term financing (typically 1-3 days) used by wholesalers to complete double-close transactions. In a double close, the wholesaler buys the property from the seller (A-to-B transaction) and immediately resells it to the end buyer (B-to-C transaction). Transactional funding provides the capital for the A-to-B side, and the funds from the B-to-C sale repay the loan -- often within the same day. This allows wholesalers to complete deals where assignment contracts aren't possible or where they want to keep their profit margin private.
bestFor: Real estate wholesalers doing double closes
Deals where assignment isn't allowed or preferred
Wholesalers who want to keep margins private
notGoodFor: Deals without a confirmed end buyer
Long-term holds of any kind
Fix and flip projects
Deals where B-to-C closing is uncertain or delayed
Investors who only do assignments
qualificationRequirements: Signed A-to-B purchase agreement (with seller)
Signed B-to-C purchase agreement (with end buyer)
Proof of end buyer's funds or financing approval
Title company willing to facilitate double close
Clear title on the property
End buyer closing must be confirmed and imminent

pros: 100% financing -- no money out of pocket
Close in the same day as end buyer
Keep profit margins completely private
No credit check or income verification
Simple and fast process once set up
Unlimited deal volume possible
cons: Only works for double-close transactions
Requires confirmed end buyer before funding
Fees of 1-2% reduce profit margin
Title company must agree to double close
Very short window -- timing is critical
Not all markets or title companies support this

howToApply: Secure your deal: get the A-to-B contract signed with the seller
Find and confirm your end buyer: get the B-to-C contract signed with proof of funds
Contact a transactional funding provider and submit both contracts
Coordinate with a title company experienced in double closes
Fund A-to-B close with transactional lender's capital
Complete B-to-C close (same day or next day) -- transactional lender is repaid from proceeds

name: HELOC for Real Estate Investing
h1: Using a HELOC for Real Estate Investing -- Tap Your Home Equity
description: How to use a home equity line of credit (HELOC) for real estate investing. Strategy, risks, rates, and when it makes sense to invest with HELOC funds.
keywords: HELOC real estate investing, home equity line of credit investing, HELOC for down payment, HELOC investment property, HELOC strategy real estate
overview: A Home Equity Line of Credit (HELOC) allows homeowners to borrow against the equity in their primary residence or existing investment property. For real estate investors, a HELOC provides a revolving line of credit that can be used as a down payment on investment properties, to fund renovations, or even to purchase properties outright in lower-priced markets. The key advantage is that you only pay interest on the amount drawn, and you can reuse the line as you pay it down -- making it a powerful tool for recycling capital across multiple deals.
bestFor: Funding down payments on investment properties
Bridge financing while waiting for refinance
notGoodFor: Those with little home equity
Risk-averse homeowners (your home is collateral)
High-interest-rate environments
Funding deals with thin margins
Borrowers already at high DTI ratios

typicalTerms.loanToValue: Up to 80-90% CLTV on primary residence

qualificationRequirements: Sufficient home equity (typically need 20%+ remaining after HELOC)
Credit score 680+ for best terms
DTI ratio under 43% including HELOC payment
Stable income documentation
Property appraisal to confirm current value
Primary residence or qualifying investment property as collateral

pros: Only pay interest on what you draw
Revolving line -- reuse as you pay down
Can fund down payments, rehab, or full purchases
Interest may be tax deductible (consult CPA)
No usage restrictions on investment type
Relatively low closing costs to open
cons: Variable rate -- payments can increase
Your home is collateral -- risk of foreclosure
Adds to your DTI which can limit future financing
Draw period eventually ends (forced repayment)
Lenders can freeze or reduce lines in downturns
Interest rate is higher than primary mortgage rate

howToApply: Calculate your available equity: current home value minus mortgage balance
Check your credit score and DTI ratio -- aim for 680+ and sub-43% DTI
Shop HELOC rates with 3-5 lenders: local banks, credit unions, and online lenders
Apply with documentation: income, tax returns, mortgage statements, home insurance
Complete home appraisal and underwriting
Open the line -- draw funds as needed for investment deals

name: Portfolio Loans
h1: Portfolio Loans for Real Estate Investors -- Beyond Conventional Limits
description: Complete guide to portfolio loans for real estate investors. How portfolio lenders work, qualification requirements, and when to use them over conventional.
keywords: portfolio loans, portfolio lender real estate, portfolio mortgage, non-conforming loan, local bank investment loan, community bank real estate loan
overview: Portfolio loans are mortgages that the lender keeps on their own books rather than selling to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Because the lender retains the loan, they set their own underwriting criteria -- meaning they can be more flexible on credit scores, DTI ratios, number of properties, and property types. Portfolio lenders are typically local banks, community banks, and credit unions. For real estate investors who have exceeded the 10-property conventional loan limit or who don't fit standard underwriting boxes, portfolio loans are often the best path to continued growth.
bestFor: Borrowers who don't fit conventional guidelines
Experienced investors with strong net worth
notGoodFor: Rate shoppers seeking the absolute lowest rate
Those who want standardized, predictable terms
National or remote investing (portfolio lenders prefer local)
New investors with no portfolio or net worth
Properties outside the lender's geographic area
qualificationRequirements: Strong relationship with local bank or credit union
Solid net worth and liquidity demonstration
Portfolio of existing performing properties (track record)
20-30% down payment
Credit score 660+ (varies by lender)
Personal financial statement and tax returns

pros: No limit on number of financed properties
Flexible underwriting beyond Fannie/Freddie rules
Relationship-based -- can negotiate terms
Can finance unique property types
Local decision-making (not corporate underwriting)
Potential for cross-collateralization and portfolio discounts
cons: Higher rates than conventional loans
Shorter terms and balloon payments common
Prepayment penalties often apply
Availability depends on local market and lender
May require larger down payments (25-30%)
Less standardized -- each lender is different

howToApply: Identify local and community banks and credit unions that portfolio their loans
Prepare a personal financial statement with complete asset and liability schedule
Document your real estate portfolio: properties owned, values, rents, and performance
Schedule meetings with commercial loan officers (relationship matters)
Present your deal or portfolio refinance opportunity
Negotiate terms, complete underwriting, and close

name: Commercial Real Estate Loans
h1: Commercial Real Estate Loans -- Financing 5+ Unit Properties & Mixed-Use
description: Guide to commercial real estate loans for investors. Financing for 5+ unit multifamily, mixed-use, office, retail, and industrial properties.
keywords: commercial real estate loans, commercial mortgage, multifamily loan, 5 unit loan, commercial property financing, CRE loans
overview: Commercial real estate loans finance properties with 5 or more residential units, or any property used for commercial purposes (office, retail, industrial, mixed-use). Unlike residential loans that focus on the borrower's personal income, commercial loans underwrite primarily based on the property's Net Operating Income (NOI) and Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR). These loans are structured differently from residential mortgages -- they typically have shorter terms, balloon payments, and recourse provisions. For investors scaling beyond the 4-unit residential threshold, understanding commercial lending is essential.
notGoodFor: 1-4 unit residential properties (use residential loans)
New investors with no commercial experience
Properties with vacancy or unstable income
Quick closings (commercial underwriting is thorough)
Small loan amounts under $250K (overhead too high)
qualificationRequirements: Property DSCR of 1.20-1.35 minimum
20-35% down payment (varies by property type and lender)
Borrower net worth equal to or exceeding loan amount
Commercial real estate experience (preferred)
Environmental phase 1 assessment
Full property financials: rent roll, P&L, operating statements

pros: Finance larger and more profitable properties
Income-based underwriting (property cash flow focused)
Higher leverage potential on performing assets
Can structure as non-recourse on larger deals
SBA loans available for owner-occupied commercial
Significant tax advantages (depreciation, cost segregation)
cons: More complex underwriting and documentation
Higher closing costs and legal fees
Shorter terms with balloon payments
Prepayment penalties can be expensive
Personal guarantees often required on smaller deals
Requires commercial lending experience to navigate

howToApply: Prepare a full property analysis: rent roll, operating expenses, NOI, DSCR
Create a borrower package: personal financial statement, net worth, liquidity, experience resume
Identify appropriate lenders: CMBS, life insurance, banks, SBA, credit unions
Submit loan applications to 3-5 lenders with your property and borrower packages
Review and negotiate term sheets -- focus on rate, term, prepayment, and recourse
Complete due diligence: appraisal, environmental, survey, title, and close

name: Bridge Loans
h1: Bridge Loans for Real Estate Investors -- Short-Term Capital Solutions
description: How bridge loans work for real estate investors. Rates, terms, use cases, and how to use bridge financing for acquisitions and value-add projects.
keywords: bridge loans real estate, bridge financing, bridge loan rates, short term real estate loan, bridge lender, gap financing real estate
overview: Bridge loans are short-term financing designed to 'bridge' the gap between buying a property and securing permanent financing or selling it. They are commonly used by investors who need to close quickly on an acquisition while arranging long-term debt, or who are completing a value-add project (renovations, lease-up) before refinancing into permanent financing. Bridge loans are similar to hard money loans but are often available at slightly better terms for stabilized or near-stabilized properties. They are essential for investors who need speed and flexibility.
bestFor: Gap financing between purchase and permanent loan
Investors waiting to season a property for DSCR refinance
notGoodFor: Stabilized buy-and-hold (use DSCR or conventional)
Borrowers with no clear exit strategy
Properties with no path to permanent financing
Tight-margin deals where bridge costs erode profit
Long-term holds exceeding 24 months

typicalTerms.prepaymentPenalty: Varies -- many have none after 6 months

qualificationRequirements: Property as primary collateral
Clear exit strategy (refinance into permanent debt or sell)
10-25% down payment or equity position
Basic borrower financials (net worth, liquidity)
Property appraisal or valuation
Real estate investing experience (preferred for larger deals)

pros: Fast closing in 7-21 days
Flexible on property condition and type
Can finance value-add and transitional properties
Bridge to permanent financing at better terms
Less borrower-focused underwriting than conventional
Interest-only payments keep carrying costs manageable
cons: Higher interest rates than permanent financing
Origination points add upfront cost
Short term creates refinance or sale pressure
Must have a viable exit strategy
Can be expensive if project timeline extends
Variable terms across lenders -- shop carefully

howToApply: Define your exit strategy clearly: will you refinance into DSCR/conventional or sell?
Prepare property analysis: current value, projected value after improvements, timeline
Contact 3-5 bridge lenders and request term sheets
Compare terms: rate, points, LTV (as-is vs. ARV), term, extension options, prepayment
Select lender and submit full application with property and borrower documentation
Complete appraisal, underwriting, and close at title company
